subject: Change getCanonicalName() to getName() in drools-pdp
commit_message:
Change getCanonicalName() to getName() in drools-pdp

Per javadocs, getName() should generally be used instead of
Class.getCanonicalName(). This change only applies to classes; it
does not apply to File objects.

Change-Id: I28df56b3dfd0382239960d7f0f6e1131d702b2ab
Issue-ID: POLICY-1646
Signed-off-by: Jim Hahn <jrh3@att.com>

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-drools-pdp-master-verify-java/819/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/policy-drools-pdp-master-verify-java/819

Patch Set 1:

Can you verify in drools-applications that we don't have any instance of nested classes being referred in the properties configuration?   I understand that

getName() will use "x.y.z$NestedClass" and getCanonicalName() would be "x.y.z.NestedClass".

The question is if those classes are passed through "Class.forName(..)" to instantiate them, which format does Class.forName(..) understand for nested classes, the one with "." or the one with "$" or both ?

Patch Set 1:

> Can you verify in drools-applications that we don't have any
 > instance of nested classes being referred in the properties
 > configuration?   I understand that
 > 
 > getName() will use "x.y.z$NestedClass" and getCanonicalName() would
 > be "x.y.z.NestedClass".
 > 
 > The question is if those classes are passed through
 > "Class.forName(..)" to instantiate them, which format does
 > Class.forName(..) understand for nested classes, the one with "."
 > or the one with "$" or both ?

Class.forName() works with getName(), so should be OK.  It does not work with getCanonicalName() (unless it's the same as getName()).

Patch Set 1:

> > Can you verify in drools-applications that we don't have any
 > > instance of nested classes being referred in the properties
 > > configuration?   I understand that
 > >
 > > getName() will use "x.y.z$NestedClass" and getCanonicalName()
 > would
 > > be "x.y.z.NestedClass".
 > >
 > > The question is if those classes are passed through
 > > "Class.forName(..)" to instantiate them, which format does
 > > Class.forName(..) understand for nested classes, the one with "."
 > > or the one with "$" or both ?
 > 
 > Class.forName() works with getName(), so should be OK.  It does not
 > work with getCanonicalName() (unless it's the same as getName()).

As reference, here's a snippet from the javadoc:

public static Class<?> forName(String name,
                               boolean initialize,
                               ClassLoader loader)
                        throws ClassNotFoundException

Returns the Class object associated with the class or interface with the given string name, using the given class loader. Given the fully qualified name for a class or interface (in the same format returned by getName)...
Change has been successfully merged by Jorge Hernandez

Unable to build due to a non-resolvable parent POM. Search logs for "[FATAL] Non-resolvable parent POM" to find the logs.

The fix for this is usually to make sure your poms are pointing to parents that exist. Double check:

 * groupId is correct and no typos
 * artifactId is correct and no typos
 * version is correct for branch ( https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-drools-pdp-master-merge-java/4/ )

There are test failures. Please look in the log to identify them. ( https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-drools-pdp-master-merge-java/4/ )
